meta.question: How can we build a live visualization of Wheat that teaches the framework by showing itself in action?

claims.content: The visualization must be built through the Wheat pipeline itself — claims, compiler, then artifact. It should demonstrate the system by being a product of it.
Output must be self-contained HTML (inline CSS/JS, no external dependencies) that can be opened directly in a browser.
Target audience is a single developer seeing Wheat for the first time. The visualization should be self-explanatory without external docs.
Full creative freedom — no budget, timeline, or tech constraints beyond self-contained HTML. Go as ambitious as needed.
Success criteria is subjective — 'I'll know it when I see it.' The artifact must be visually compelling enough to sell itself on first impression.

Use a tiny Node.js server (~80 lines, zero dependencies) with Server-Sent Events (SSE) to push compilation updates to the browser in real time. SSE beats WebSockets here: one-way data flow, auto-reconnect built into the browser, and zero npm dependencies.
Node.js fs.watch uses OS-level file notifications (FSEvents on macOS, inotify on Linux). For watching a single file like claims.json, it is sufficient without chokidar. Zero dependencies needed.
Browser-only approaches (File System Access API) are not viable: Chrome-only, require manual file picker every session, and still need a server to trigger CLI commands. A server is unavoidable.

Use AST Explorer's pattern: input on left (claims flowing in), compiled output on right (compilation result), updating in real time as data changes. This is the closest proven analogue to showing claims.json -> compiler -> compilation.json.

For the compiler passes, use a linear pipeline stepper (like GitHub Actions) — stages shown left-to-right with status icons (pending/running/done/error). Color coding: green=pass, yellow=running, red=fail, grey=pending.

Next.js HMR pattern: server sends lightweight notification with just a hash, browser fetches full data only when needed. For Wheat: SSE sends {event:'update', hash:'abc'}, client fetches /compilation.json. Avoids sending large payloads over SSE.

A live-updating UI requires a running server, which conflicts with the 'self-contained HTML' constraint (d002). Resolution: the server is the dev experience; the HTML it serves is still self-contained. Two artifacts: wheat-server.js (dev tool) + output HTML (portable artifact).

The entire live server can be built with zero npm dependencies — Node.js core http + fs modules only. Estimated ~80-100 lines for: static file serving, SSE endpoint, file watcher, and compiler auto-run.

Working prototype: wheat-server.js is 133 lines (not ~80 as estimated). Still zero npm dependencies. Includes: HTTP server, SSE endpoint, fs.watch on claims.json, auto-compile, static file serving, git log endpoint.
SSE auto-reconnect confirmed working: EventSource reconnects automatically when server restarts, no client-side retry logic needed. Browser handles it natively.

Split-pane layout works: left panel shows claims grouped by phase with type/evidence badges, right panel shows compiled output (coverage bars, conflicts, warnings, git log, certificate). Compiler stepper animates across the top on each update.
fs.watch fires reliably on macOS for claims.json edits. 150ms debounce is sufficient to avoid double-fires. Compiler runs in ~50ms for 13 claims — instant feedback loop.

The live dashboard successfully visualizes its own sprint. As prototype claims were added to claims.json, the dashboard updated in real time showing the new claims, updated coverage bars, and re-animated compiler stepper. Self-referential loop confirmed working.

User prefers the static file:// dashboard (output/dashboard.html) over the live SSE server (localhost:3141). The SSE server doesn't add enough value — most interaction is reading the current state, which the static HTML already shows well. The live server is over-engineered for the actual use pattern.

The self-contained HTML approach (file:// openable, no server needed) is actually the right call for this use case. The /status command already regenerates the dashboard on demand — that's sufficient. Real-time SSE updates are cool but unnecessary.

The live SSE server IS valuable as a dev-time tool — watching claims update in real time during a sprint is useful. The earlier preference for static (f001) was about the final artifact, not the dev experience. Both have a role: live server for working, static HTML for sharing.

The live dashboard UI (localhost:3141) is too rudimentary — functional but not sleek. Needs a UX polish pass: the layout, typography, and visual hierarchy should feel as polished as the static /status dashboard, not like a debug tool.

The dashboard needs a timeline-based UI — a visual narrative of the sprint's progression over time, not just a static grid of current state. Data-rich dashboards can be built without overwhelming the user by using progressive disclosure, timeline views, and visual hierarchy. The current layout dumps everything at once instead of telling a story.

Timeline-first dashboard prototype built: vertical timeline as primary UI spine, claims as expandable nodes grouped by phase (define/research/prototype/evaluate/feedback), progressive disclosure via click-to-expand detail panels. Compact scoreboard replaces the old card grid. Coverage shown as inline topic chips with evidence-tier colored dots. Conflict resolutions and warnings appear as inline timeline events. 'Now' node pulses at the bottom showing live watching state.

Phase-colored visual system confirmed effective: each sprint phase gets a distinct color (define=amber, research=blue, prototype=green, evaluate=purple, feedback=cyan) applied consistently to timeline dots, claim IDs, phase headers, and claim dot accents. Creates instant visual grouping without labels.

New information (latest claims, events, compilations) should populate from the top of the timeline, not the bottom. Reverse chronological order — newest first, like a social feed or activity log. The user shouldn't have to scroll to see what just changed.

3 of 4 'weak evidence' compiler warnings are false alarms. Topics audience, scope, and quality consist entirely of constraint/feedback claims whose natural evidence tier is 'stated'. Constraints are stakeholder decisions, not testable hypotheses — 'stated' is their correct and final tier. The compiler's evidence tier system was designed for testable claims, not definitional boundaries.

The output-format topic is the only real evidence gap among the 4 warnings. It has a risk claim (r007) at web tier about server-vs-static tension. This is upgradeable to 'tested' by verifying that output/dashboard.html works as self-contained HTML: opens via file://, zero external network requests, all CSS/JS inline, works offline.

The compiler's W_WEAK_EVIDENCE warning system has a semantic blind spot: it treats all claim types equally, but constraint claims (type: constraint, feedback) are inherently 'stated' tier and cannot be upgraded. This creates persistent false-positive warnings on constraint-heavy topics, reducing signal-to-noise ratio for future sprints.

The compiler should add constraint-aware coverage analysis: topics where >50% of claims are type 'constraint' or 'feedback' should not emit W_WEAK_EVIDENCE. Instead, emit W_CONSTRAINT_ONLY as an informational note. This preserves the warning for genuinely under-researched topics while silencing noise on definitional ones.

All 4 static HTML artifacts (output/dashboard.html, output/problem-statement.html, research/live-ui-architecture.html, research/evidence-gap-analysis.html) verified fully self-contained: zero external URLs, zero
